tweets.text:
"I've known Marc and Ben for a long time, and we got oh-so-close to partnering up at Uber in 2011. I blame Marc, Ben blames himself but let's just say it was on all of us and in 2017 Uber suffered the consequences of not having Marc on the board - iykyk."

7 minutes of Travis Kalanick and Ben Horowitz on how the story went down from both sides:

"I have this whole fundraising process that I would do just to perfection, and that means I'd run an auction."

"And I would do this thing where I would say, 'We don't know, this is gonna be one of the hottest deals in Silicon Valley this year. We don't know where the price is gonna be, where it's gonna go to, but it's at least blank.'"

"And I call this the uncapped anchor, meaning I'm never in a place where I'm negotiating with somebody where I'm here and they're here and we meet in the middle. I'm always saying, 'It's at least this, and it can go up.'"

"We got to 375 million pre with a16z."

Ben: "I was like, 'F***, I know we should do that deal. We lost the deal.'"
tweets.full_text:
"I've known Marc and Ben for a long time, and we got oh-so-close to partnering up at Uber in 2011. I blame Marc, Ben blames himself but let's just say it was on all of us and in 2017 Uber suffered the consequences of not having Marc on the board - iykyk."

7 minutes of Travis Kalanick and Ben Horowitz on how the story went down from both sides:

"I have this whole fundraising process that I would do just to perfection, and that means I'd run an auction."

"And I would do this thing where I would say, 'We don't know, this is gonna be one of the hottest deals in Silicon Valley this year. We don't know where the price is gonna be, where it's gonna go to, but it's at least blank.'"

"And I call this the uncapped anchor, meaning I'm never in a place where I'm negotiating with somebody where I'm here and they're here and we meet in the middle. I'm always saying, 'It's at least this, and it can go up.'"

"We got to 375 million pre with a16z."

Ben: "I was like, 'F***, I know we should do that deal. We lost the deal.'"

tweets.text:
BREAKING: US job postings on Indeed fell -3.5% YoY in the week ending July 10th, to near the lowest this year.

Postings have declined -37.2% since April 2022 and are now down to February 2021 levels.

The number of available vacancies in the US is now only +1.0% above pre-pandemic levels recorded in February 2020.

Furthermore, new job postings on Indeed dropped -10.9% YoY, to the lowest since early January, continuing their over 4-year decline.

As a result, new job postings are now -3.5% below February 2020 levels.

This all suggests a further deterioration in official BLS job openings data for June and July.

The US labor market is losing momentum.

As a result, new job postings are now -3.5% below February 2020 levels.

The US labor market is losing momentum.

tweets.text:
"In 2022, months before ChatGPT and years before Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex, Gavin Uberti, Robert Wachen, and Chris Zhu dropped out of Harvard to make a bet that looked, at the time, nearly irresponsible: they would build an entire inference system—new chips, boards, interconnects, racks—from scratch."

"What began as a radical, contrarian wager has matured into a strong bet on the defining challenge of our era."

"In a world where production is the product and the machine is the moat, we’re thrilled to partner with Etched."

a16z's Sarah Wang and Raghu Raghuram on why AI inference may be the most consequential workload of our lifetime, and how @Etched is betting on it: https://t.co/hO7sfNKbjI

tweets.text:
BREAKING: President Trump says he is "close" to a decision on a "massive attack" on Iran, per Axios.

Details include:

1. President Trump says "we are all set for it" regarding the attack which he has been hinting at for weeks

2. President Trump says this attack would be "bigger than ever before"

3. President Trump said Israel "would join in two minutes if I ask them to," but added that "we don't need anybody"

"They haven't received enough pain yet," Trump says.

ARK Invest is a proud member of the Bitcoin Security Consortium @BTCconsortium! Together with @Anchorage @BlackRock @blocks @Blockstream @coinbase @DigitalAssets @galaxyhq and @Strategy, we believe disruptive innovation demands proactive security and a well-informed investor base.

As the industry begins preparing bitcoin for a post-quantum future, we believe the core goals of this initiative carry outsized impact: funding bitcoin developers and keeping the public informed on the state of quantum computing and how the community is working to secure the network's long-term future.

tweets.text: Looking into this more there is def a correlation bt $BITO volume spikes and $IBIT price reversals up, which would seem to indicate the basis trade heating up given's BITO's explosive volume the past few weeks. Bc again, no regular investor is going to use BITO which charges 95bps and tracks futures when you can buy spot etfs for 20bps. So it pretty much has to be pro traders a la HFs in an arb trade. Not 100% sure on this but seems like a logical theory..
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Weird.. $BITO is 6th most traded ETF today. And in July it's traded $40b vs $20b for the Mighty IBIT.  Very unusual given IBIT dwarfed it at one point bc ETF invs much prefer spot to futures when given choice. Basis trade can't explain it, could be some other arb tho? https://t.co/ldkYZRrhse
tweets.full_text: Looking into this more there is def a correlation bt $BITO volume spikes and $IBIT price reversals up, which would seem to indicate the basis trade heating up given's BITO's explosive volume the past few weeks. Bc again, no regular investor is going to use BITO which charges 95bps and tracks futures when you can buy spot etfs for 20bps. So it pretty much has to be pro traders a la HFs in an arb trade. Not 100% sure on this but seems like a logical theory..

thread_tweets.text: @user889889 Unlikely. Ultra-long bonds are extremely rate sensitive. The price drop is mostly duration + rates. GOOGL just raised capex again last night due to strong demand and incremental profitability.

tweets.text:
🚨🛢️🚢Wow — I just learned that in AIS ship-tracking data on @TheTerminal  you can watch crude oil tanker captains sending messages to the Houthis through the vessel's "Destination" field.

Instead of a port, they're broadcasting things like "China Crew & Owner," "Armed Guards O/B," "Security Guard On Bd," or "For Orders" — basically signaling "don't shoot, we're not a target." Right now two VLCCs are broadcasting that they have Chinese crews and owners.

Pretty amazing to see geopolitics play out one AIS field at a time. A sample of what's transiting right now:

*IB me for a link to this specific view.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: 🛢️Just added Bab el-Mandeb to my real-time @TheTerminal chart — now tracking tanker crossings at both chokepoints (Hormuz + Bab el-Mandeb) against Brent: https://t.co/1CJQLrnbb2

thread_tweets.text:
That's the conventional wisdom...and is true sometimes.

But, in this regime, the paper market is dictating direction. Once managed money short % got up to 42%, it was obvious that a rally was coming.

We all would have told ourselves a story for why the rally happened (undervalued, inventories, rebalancing, geopol, etc.). Mostly pretext.

Just my 2cents...I won't be long anymore once the positioning flips/shorts all cover.

tweets.text:
First Principles Ep. 6 with Ron Rivest

Long before the internet became a place to bank, transact, or build blockchains, Turing Award winner Ron Rivest helped solve a foundational problem: how can strangers communicate securely without first meeting to exchange a secret?

Rivest tells the story of inventing RSA with Adi Shamir and Leonard Adleman, why digital signatures fascinated him even more than encryption, and how cryptographic hashes, government pressure, patents, and standards shaped the security infrastructure we rely on today.

Rivest shares his unusually candid views on quantum computing, post-quantum security, and more.

Hosted by @Tim_Roughgarden with @danboneh.

00:00 Intro: the two problems that could break modern cryptography
01:10 Why Ron Rivest’s work underpins the internet and blockchains
08:10 Before public-key cryptography, there was no theory of security
11:05 The open problem that led to RSA
13:55 The night Ron Rivest discovered the core idea behind RSA
17:55 Why digital signatures were the real breakthrough
19:14 The RSA challenge — and a prediction that was off by quadrillions of years
28:33 How government pressure shaped cryptographic standards
30:36 Designing the hash functions that made digital signatures practical
33:26 The Fiat–Shamir transformation, explained
38:04 Building a cryptography company before the web existed
42:31 Will quantum computers ever become powerful enough to break RSA?
48:02 The cryptography securing the internet, elections, and everyday life
50:11 What surprised Dan: Quantum giveth and quantum taketh away
tweets.full_text:
First Principles Ep. 6 with Ron Rivest

Long before the internet became a place to bank, transact, or build blockchains, Turing Award winner Ron Rivest helped solve a foundational problem: how can strangers communicate securely without first meeting to exchange a secret?

Rivest tells the story of inventing RSA with Adi Shamir and Leonard Adleman, why digital signatures fascinated him even more than encryption, and how cryptographic hashes, government pressure, patents, and standards shaped the security infrastructure we rely on today.

Rivest shares his unusually candid views on quantum computing, post-quantum security, and more.

Hosted by @Tim_Roughgarden with @danboneh.

00:00 Intro: the two problems that could break modern cryptography
01:10 Why Ron Rivest’s work underpins the internet and blockchains
08:10 Before public-key cryptography, there was no theory of security
11:05 The open problem that led to RSA
13:55 The night Ron Rivest discovered the core idea behind RSA
17:55 Why digital signatures were the real breakthrough
19:14 The RSA challenge — and a prediction that was off by quadrillions of years
28:33 How government pressure shaped cryptographic standards
30:36 Designing the hash functions that made digital signatures practical
33:26 The Fiat–Shamir transformation, explained
38:04 Building a cryptography company before the web existed
42:31 Will quantum computers ever become powerful enough to break RSA?
48:02 The cryptography securing the internet, elections, and everyday life
50:11 What surprised Dan: Quantum giveth and quantum taketh away

tweets.text: Tesla revenues hit a record $28 billion in Q2, rising 26% over the last year. That was the highest revenue growth since Q2 2023. Net Income came in below expectations at $1.1 billion, a 5% decline from a year ago. Operating margins fell to 1.4% from 4.1% a year ago. Free cash flow turned negative (-$1.1 billion) due to a spike in capital expenditures (up 142% YoY). $TSLA
tweets.full_text: Tesla revenues hit a record $28 billion in Q2, rising 26% over the last year. That was the highest revenue growth since Q2 2023. Net Income came in below expectations at $1.1 billion, a 5% decline from a year ago. Operating margins fell to 1.4% from 4.1% a year ago. Free cash flow turned negative (-$1.1 billion) due to a spike in capital expenditures (up 142% YoY). $TSLA

tweets.text: Tesla delivered 480k vehicles in Q2, up 25% from a year ago (384k). This was the highest YoY growth rate since Q3 2023. $TSLA https://t.co/QGrVpyeqDr

tweets.text: 87% of Google's record $112 billion net income in the 2nd quarter came from historic markups in its equity investments (SpaceX/Anthropic/etc). Without this "other income" Google's net income would have shown a modest 2% increase from Q1 to roughly $33 billion. $GOOGL https://t.co/cwVD3ugnMP

tweets.text: Google reported negative free cash flow of $5.9 billion in Q2, driven by massive capital expenditures on AI infrastructure. This marks the first time in the company's public history that its quarterly free cash flow turned negative. $GOOGL https://t.co/v1r7DoOvBj
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Google's quarterly spending on Property & Equipment (AI Infrastructure) has doubled over the past year to $44.9 billion. They now expect capex for the year of $195-$205 billion, up from last quarter's forecast of $180-$190 billion. $GOOGL https://t.co/DoKtBVlu1K

tweets.text:
The oil market and energy stocks are finally pointing the same way.

Energy stocks in uptrends outnumber those in downtrends by more than 50 points, and crude's front month is more than 5% above the deferred contract.
$XLE was higher 71% of the time 4 to 6 months later. Median gain over the next year, 7%.

Energy did not lead the S&P 500 after these, and crude's own returns were mixed.
The statistical edge here is thin.

Read full analysis: https://t.co/VcG72vvJUA
